CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the writing quality is reported as good, with clear exposition of an MDL formulation, an EM-style algorithm, and experiments on Slavic datasets. Main contributions are stated early and related work coverage is adequate, and notation appears consistent enough to follow the method/shortest-path formulation. Figures/tables (e.g. sDL/NCD references) and examples of discovered correspondences are informative, but minor presentation gaps remain—e.g. no mention of code or hyperparameters and limited detail on algorithmic complexity in the prose—which slightly reduces reproducibility and clarity from a rigorous-methods perspective. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states the MDL-based contributions early, which suits my applied-research perspective. Notation and methods are generally clear and the use of examples (e.g. shortest-path reduction, NCD table) helps make the algorithmic procedure interpretable, but a few practical reporting omissions (no hyperparameter details, code release not mentioned) and the stated implementation bottlenecks could hinder reproducibility. Writing quality is good and related work coverage adequate, so only minor presentation and reporting improvements are needed for full clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the writing quality is reported as good, with clear exposition of an MDL formulation, an EM-style algorithm, and experiments on Slavic datasets. Main contributions are stated early and related work coverage is adequate, and notation appears consistent enough to follow the method/shortest-path formulation. Figures/tables (e.g. sDL/NCD references) and examples of discovered correspondences are informative, but minor presentation gaps remain—e.g. no mention of code or hyperparameters and limited detail on algorithmic complexity in the prose—which slightly reduces reproducibility and clarity from a rigorous-methods perspective.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states the MDL-based contributions early, which suits my applied-research perspective. Notation and methods are generally clear and the use of examples (e.g. shortest-path reduction, NCD table) helps make the algorithmic procedure interpretable, but a few practical reporting omissions (no hyperparameter details, code release not mentioned) and the stated implementation bottlenecks could hinder reproducibility. Writing quality is good and related work coverage adequate, so only minor presentation and reporting improvements are needed for full clarity.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=1] A(1): The paper reports no baseline comparisons (Baselines compared: none mentioned) despite evaluating on standard Swadesh and cognate sets, so it fails to situate its MDL method against established alternatives. From my MDL-focused perspective, lacking comparisons to principled baselines (e.g., Bayesian, compression- or alignment-based methods) and no fair head-to-head evaluation undermines claims of improvement. Although related work coverage is reported as adequate, the empirical section does not acknowledge when prior methods might perform comparably or better, nor does it provide controlled, same-data metric comparisons. | B(1): The paper includes no empirical baselines—no prior methods are evaluated alongside the MDL method—so there are no direct, fair comparisons using the same data or metrics. Although evaluation uses MDL/sDL and references NCD, without competing systems or reported baselines the authors cannot demonstrate superiority or parity, nor acknowledge when prior methods might perform comparably. While related work coverage is described as adequate, the lack of any baseline experiments is a critical omission for assessing comparative performance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports no baseline comparisons (Baselines compared: none mentioned) despite evaluating on standard Swadesh and cognate sets, so it fails to situate its MDL method against established alternatives. From my MDL-focused perspective, lacking comparisons to principled baselines (e.g., Bayesian, compression- or alignment-based methods) and no fair head-to-head evaluation undermines claims of improvement. Although related work coverage is reported as adequate, the empirical section does not acknowledge when prior methods might perform comparably or better, nor does it provide controlled, same-data metric comparisons.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper includes no empirical baselines—no prior methods are evaluated alongside the MDL method—so there are no direct, fair comparisons using the same data or metrics. Although evaluation uses MDL/sDL and references NCD, without competing systems or reported baselines the authors cannot demonstrate superiority or parity, nor acknowledge when prior methods might perform comparably. While related work coverage is described as adequate, the lack of any baseline experiments is a critical omission for assessing comparative performance.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important problem—automatic discovery of multilingual word correspondences—using a principled MDL formulation and an efficient EM-style algorithm (E-step as shortest-path, M-step merging) which could influence methodology in computational historical linguistics. However, the lack of baselines, formal identifiability or convergence guarantees, and reliance on subjective data over-weighting and a small Swadesh dataset limit immediate practical uptake and reproducibility. The clear algorithmic mapping and demonstration on Slavic languages open plausible new research directions (scalability, theory for weighting, and broader language families), but because key theoretical and empirical validations are missing, I view the impact as useful but not field-changing. | B(3): The paper tackles an important task—automatic discovery of cross-lingual correspondences—with a principled MDL formulation and an efficient EM/shortest-path algorithm that enables interpretable multi-language rules and empirical discoveries on 13 Slavic languages. Its practical value is clear for corpus-driven linguistic analysis (novel interpretable outputs, quantification of shared structure via sDL/NCD), but impact is limited by scalability and data-size constraints, lack of baselines and unclear code release, and evaluations confined to relatively small Swadesh/cognate sets. Overall, it opens useful research directions for multilingual discovery and applied historical/typological work but is unlikely to be immediately transformative without broader validation and tooling to scale to more languages/data.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—automatic discovery of multilingual word correspondences—using a principled MDL formulation and an efficient EM-style algorithm (E-step as shortest-path, M-step merging) which could influence methodology in computational historical linguistics. However, the lack of baselines, formal identifiability or convergence guarantees, and reliance on subjective data over-weighting and a small Swadesh dataset limit immediate practical uptake and reproducibility. The clear algorithmic mapping and demonstration on Slavic languages open plausible new research directions (scalability, theory for weighting, and broader language families), but because key theoretical and empirical validations are missing, I view the impact as useful but not field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important task—automatic discovery of cross-lingual correspondences—with a principled MDL formulation and an efficient EM/shortest-path algorithm that enables interpretable multi-language rules and empirical discoveries on 13 Slavic languages. Its practical value is clear for corpus-driven linguistic analysis (novel interpretable outputs, quantification of shared structure via sDL/NCD), but impact is limited by scalability and data-size constraints, lack of baselines and unclear code release, and evaluations confined to relatively small Swadesh/cognate sets. Overall, it opens useful research directions for multilingual discovery and applied historical/typological work but is unlikely to be immediately transformative without broader validation and tooling to scale to more languages/data.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper lists datasets (Swadesh lists from Wiktionary and a Slavic cognates set) and describes the MDL/EM-style algorithm and metrics (MDL, sDL, NCD), so a skilled researcher could partially reimplement it. However, key reproducibility details are missing: hyperparameters and training/configuration details are not reported, the data preprocessing pipeline is not clearly specified, and no code is provided. These omissions (plus the subjective data over-weighting mechanism lacking principled specification) make full reproduction difficult without author clarification. | B(3): Partially reproducible. The paper gives a clear high-level algorithmic description (MDL objective, EM-style loop, shortest-path/Dijkstra alignment) and specifies corpora sources (Swadesh lists from Wiktionary and a Slavic cognates set), which should allow reimplementation in principle. However, key experimental details are missing: hyperparameters and model/configuration choices are not reported, preprocessing pipeline is not fully described, code is not released, and the evaluation metrics/procedures (MDL/sDL/NCD) are not specified to the level needed for exact replication. These gaps mean a motivated implementer could reproduce approximate behavior but not faithfully replicate results without additional information.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper lists datasets (Swadesh lists from Wiktionary and a Slavic cognates set) and describes the MDL/EM-style algorithm and metrics (MDL, sDL, NCD), so a skilled researcher could partially reimplement it. However, key reproducibility details are missing: hyperparameters and training/configuration details are not reported, the data preprocessing pipeline is not clearly specified, and no code is provided. These omissions (plus the subjective data over-weighting mechanism lacking principled specification) make full reproduction difficult without author clarification.

reviewer_b.justification: Partially reproducible. The paper gives a clear high-level algorithmic description (MDL objective, EM-style loop, shortest-path/Dijkstra alignment) and specifies corpora sources (Swadesh lists from Wiktionary and a Slavic cognates set), which should allow reimplementation in principle. However, key experimental details are missing: hyperparameters and model/configuration choices are not reported, preprocessing pipeline is not fully described, code is not released, and the evaluation metrics/procedures (MDL/sDL/NCD) are not specified to the level needed for exact replication. These gaps mean a motivated implementer could reproduce approximate behavior but not faithfully replicate results without additional information.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper tackles a relevant, novel problem with an interesting MDL/EM-style approach and promising empirical findings, but they also note important weaknesses: missing baselines, limited theoretical guarantees and scalability analysis, and weak reproducibility. Given these substantive methodological and evaluation gaps that limit confidence in the claims and fit for a top venue without further work, a mid-level score is appropriate while recognizing the work's potential.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a relevant NLP/ML topic (multilingual correspondence discovery using an MDL formulation and an EM-like shortest-path alignment) and would interest the venue. However, from a MDL-theory purist perspective it lacks key elements expected at a top venue: no baselines are compared, there are no formal identifiability or convergence guarantees for the EM-style procedure, and algorithmic complexity is only discussed empirically (memory/runtime bottlenecks reported) rather than analyzed. The reliance on subjective data over-weighting without principled asymptotic analysis further weakens its fit, making the submission borderline appropriate rather than a clear fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core computational-linguistics/NLP problem (discovering cross-lingual correspondences) with a novel MDL formulation and an EM-style, shortest-path based algorithm that scales to non-trivial real data (Swadesh lists for 13 Slavic languages). Its empirical discoveries and quantitative measures (sDL, MDL, NCD) will interest the NLP/ML community, though the lack of baselines, limited scalability (five languages in current implementation), and no mention of released code weaken immediate impact. Overall it is a good fit for a top venue focused on multilingual representation and corpus-driven linguistic discovery, but would benefit from stronger empirical comparisons and reproducibility to be a perfect match.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper brings a useful and concretely implemented MDL-based approach to multilingual correspondence discovery, with practical extensions (arbitrary subsets, data weighting) and an efficient EM-style reduction to shortest-path problems. While the work is not a radical theoretical breakthrough and lacks some formal guarantees or extensive baselines, its tailored formulation and algorithmic engineering represent a meaningful and reasonably novel contribution to the area, hence a slightly above-average originality score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a focused application of MDL to multilingual correspondence discovery and an EM-style algorithm that casts alignment as shortest-path problems—an interesting but not radical methodological departure from existing MDL/EM and graph-alignment techniques. The formulation of correspondences across arbitrary subsets of languages and the use of data-weighting to control granularity are useful practical additions, but they read as incremental engineering extensions rather than a fundamentally new theoretical framework. Given the lack of principled guarantees, baselines, or asymptotic analysis in the summary, the contribution is moderately novel but would not surprise experts familiar with MDL and alignment methods.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable novel perspective by applying MDL to multilingual correspondence discovery and introducing an efficient EM-style algorithm where the E-step reduces alignment to shortest-path problems—an engineeringly elegant and practically useful contribution. Allowing correspondences between arbitrary language subsets and user-controlled data over-weighting are substantive extensions beyond standard bilingual alignment approaches. While the idea of model selection via MDL is known, its tailored formulation and algorithmic reductions for multilingual lexical discovery (demonstrated on 13 Slavic languages) are sufficiently original to inform experts, though not so radical as to be a paradigm shift.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the MDL formulation and EM-style algorithm are coherent and produce plausible results, but the paper lacks formal guarantees (identifiability/convergence/complexity), rigorous experimental controls (baselines, statistical tests, hyperparameter/code disclosure), and formal justification for the data over-weighting. Given the methodological promise but insufficient theoretical and empirical validation, a middle-ground score reflects that the approach is interesting but not yet fully substantiated.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear MDL-based claims and presents an EM-style algorithm with a shortest-path E-step, but the summary lists no formal identifiability or convergence proofs and no worst-case complexity analysis; as an MDL/EM purist I must penalize the absence of such theoretical guarantees. Experimentally the work is weak: there are no principled baselines, no statistical tests or ablations isolating the impact of key choices (notably the subjective data over-weighting), and hyperparameters/code are not reported, so empirical support is limited. Several logical gaps remain—moving away from pure MDL via over-weighting is admitted but not formally justified or analysed—so while results on Slavic lists are suggestive, the technical evidence does not adequately support the broader claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a coherent MDL formulation and an efficient EM-style algorithm (E-step via shortest-path/Dijkstra, M-step merging rules) and demonstrates non-trivial discoveries on real Slavic data, so core claims are plausible. However, mathematical guarantees or detailed derivations/proofs are not reported in the summary, experimental methodology lacks baselines, statistical tests, and hyperparameter reporting, and the introduced data over-weighting is acknowledged as subjective—these weaken the empirical support and leave some logical/validation gaps. Overall the approach appears promising but the evidence and experimental controls are insufficient to fully validate all claims.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers an interesting MDL-based approach but is limited by modest experimental scope, lack of principled baselines, sparse sensitivity/ablation studies, and weak reproducibility (missing implementation/hyperparameter details). Reviewer A’s additional concerns about theoretical guarantees and complexity strengthen the assessment that the work’s empirical and theoretical substance is currently shallow, so a score of 2 reflects these substantive gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is shallow: demonstrated only on small Slavic Swadesh and cognate sets with no principled baselines (e.g., Bayesian or information‑theoretic competitors) and no hyperparameter reporting, so claims of broad MDL‑based discovery are under‑supported. Sensitivity analyses and ablations are essentially absent (the data over‑weighting knob is used but not analyzed theoretically or empirically), and there are no convergence/identifiability proofs or formal runtime complexity guarantees despite stated computational bottlenecks. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the paper does discuss limitations and failure modes, but reproduction is hampered by missing code and hyperparameter details. Given these gaps I rate the empirical/theoretical depth as shallow overall.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents an interesting MDL-based algorithm and demonstrates non-trivial discoveries on real Slavic datasets, but the experimental scope is modest: only Swadesh lists and a small cognate set are used and no baselines are compared. Sensitivity analyses are limited—data over-weighting is described and acknowledged as subjective but there are no systematic ablations or hyperparameter studies reported. Related work coverage is described as adequate and limitations/failure modes are explicitly discussed, but reproducibility is weak (implementation details, hyperparameters, and code release are not mentioned), which constrains verification and broader applicability.

main_claims: Proposes an MDL-based method to automatically discover correspondences between words across two or more languages.

Provides an efficient algorithm (EM-style alignment with shortest-path formulation) that allows correspondences between any subset of languages while controlling the level of detail via data weighting.
Demonstrates the method on Slavic-language data, discovering non-trivial associations and quantifying shared structure between language subsets.
_summary.methodology: Formulates correspondence discovery under the Minimum Description Length principle, uses an EM-like iterative procedure where the E-step aligns data (cast as shortest-path problems solved with Dijkstra) and the M-step merges compatible rules to improve the model; allows data over-weighting to control discovery granularity.
datasets: Swadesh lists for 13 modern Slavic languages (from Wiktionary)
A Slavic cognates set (internationalisms and pan-Slavic words) for Czech, Polish, Russian, and Bulgarian
metrics: Normalized Compression Distance (NCD) (referred to via 'NCD table')
limitations: Computational bottlenecks in memory and runtime; current implementation processes up to five languages within a few hours on a 4GB RAM, 2.5GHz single-core machine.
Method requires sufficient data; under-resourced or very rich languages and larger numbers of languages may exceed available data, necessitating subjective data over-weighting.
Data over-weighting moves results away from pure MDL objectivity and introduces user subjective choices.

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical MDL Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in statistical learning and information theory with extensive experience in MDL, Bayesian model selection, and provable algorithmic analysis.
traits: Penalises papers that introduce MDL/EM style algorithms without formal identifiability or convergence proofs (will deduct points for missing theoretical guarantees).
Requires explicit algorithmic complexity analysis (worst-case time/space); will lower score if only heuristic runtime claims or amortised empirical timings are given.
Demands comparison to principled baselines (e.g., Bayesian or information-theoretic models) and ablation studies that isolate contributions; will mark down papers with only ad-hoc baselines.
Skeptical of methods relying on heuristic knobs (e.g., data over-weighting) unless accompanied by principled justification or asymptotic analysis.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on formal rigor and principled justification: rewards clear theorems/analyses and penalises empirical-only or heuristic-driven work lacking theoretical grounding.

reviewer_b.name: Multilingual NLP Applied Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Senior computational linguist with a track record of cross-lingual representation learning, corpus-based discovery of linguistic patterns, and building practical multilingual tools.
traits: Rewards novel problem formulations and discovery-oriented methods that produce linguistically interpretable outputs; will upscore clear qualitative discoveries and case studies.

Prioritises practical scalability and algorithmic engineering (efficient EM-style procedures, graph/shortest-path reductions); gives credit for methods that enable new analyses on real multilingual corpora even without formal proofs.
Values empirical evidence of usefulness: will boost score if the paper demonstrates non-trivial correspondences on real languages and quantifies shared structure between language subsets.
Favors reproducibility and usability: will penalise somewhat if code/data are not released, but less harshly than lack of empirical demonstrations.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and application-focused: rewards methods that produce novel, interpretable multilingual discoveries and scale to real data; tolerates limited formal analysis if empirical impact is clear.
